payload.summary: Part 2 deliverable: Ethical Marketing Compliance rubric constructed and applied to Batch A. Rubric grounded in FTC Section 5, NAD/BBB, AMA Ethics, ICC Code, and FTC AI disclosure guidance. Five scoring dimensions (substantiation, transparency, proportionality, respect for audience, accountability). Applied to all 8 Batch A posts. Series mean: 4.4/10 — one systemic violation (zero substantiation), two systemic concerns (transparency mismatch, missing counterarguments). Remediation path identified: four actions would lift series mean to estimated 6.5–7.5.

grounding_frameworks: FTC Section 5 — substantiation doctrine, net impression test, material deception standard
NAD/BBB — claim-level substantiation, express vs implied claims
AMA Statement of Ethics — honesty, responsibility, equity, transparency, citizenship
ICC Advertising and Marketing Communications Code (2018/2024) — legal, honest, decent, truthful; identifiability; social responsibility
FTC AI disclosure guidance (2023-2026) — AI-generated content must be distinguishable from human-created material when material

dimensions.name: transparency
dimensions.framework: AMA, ICC identifiability, FTC AI guidance

classification.violation: Breach of established ethical marketing standard if challenged by informed reader or regulatory body. Must fix.
classification.concern: Defensibility risk or departure from best practice. Worth discussing. Does not reach violation threshold.

violations: Zero substantiation for ~15 material factual claims
Zero substantiation for material quantitative claims
Zero substantiation for ~10 factual claims including channel weight hierarchy
Zero substantiation for statistical claims (debt, healthcare spending, care avoidance)
Universal negative ('exists nowhere else among ratifiers') without evidence
concerns: CESCR reporting cycle factual error (5-year vs 8-year)
Structural asymmetry (180 vs 900 words on won't/would change)

violations: Zero substantiation for historical claims and ratification dates

concerns: Speculative negative about senator constituent encounters

violations: Negative claim scope exceeds described methodology
concerns: 'The correct reading is this' undercuts fair-witness posture

systemic_violations.finding: Zero inline citations across all 8 posts. Under FTC substantiation doctrine, material factual claims require reasonable basis before dissemination. The complete absence of source attribution for verifiable claims (statistics, treaty provisions, historical dates, comparative assertions) represents a systemic substantiation failure.
systemic_violations.remediation: Add sources section to each post. Inline hyperlinks preferred. Priority: UN Treaty Collection, CRS reports, BLS/EPI data, Congressional Management Foundation.

systemic_concerns.finding: Self-description as 'researcher' or 'fair-witness' in frontmatter mismatches advocacy structure (problem-urgency-action with calls-to-action) in all 8 posts. Under ICC identifiability principle, the nature and purpose of content should be apparent.
systemic_concerns.remediation: Either revise frontmatter self-description to acknowledge advocacy framing, or restructure posts to earn fair-witness label (add counterarguments, remove urgency framing).

systemic_concerns.finding: No counterarguments presented in any Batch A post. For content that makes specific factual claims to support a call to action, the consistent omission of material counterarguments (sovereignty, federalism, RUDs, domestic-law-sufficiency) reduces proportionality. The net impression test asks what a reasonable reader takes away — without counterarguments, the impression is that no credible objections exist.
systemic_concerns.remediation: Add recurring counterarguments section. This strengthens rather than weakens advocacy by demonstrating awareness of the full debate.

positive_findings: Respect for audience scores consistently high (6.5-7.5). No exploitation of fear, shame, or identity threat anywhere in the series.
AI authorship disclosed in frontmatter across all posts. Meets minimum FTC AI disclosure standard.
Accessible language throughout. Complex treaty concepts explained for general audience without condescension.
Calls to action are empowering rather than manipulative. Reader agency preserved.
remediation_priority: Estimated series mean after remediation: 6.5-7.5 (pass range)

relationship_to_ar_scores.observation: AR scores (mean 7.2) and EM scores (mean 4.4) diverge sharply. This is expected and informative: AR measures adversarial register (how combative the rhetoric is), while EM measures ethical marketing compliance (how well claims are substantiated and presented). A text can have charitable, non-adversarial rhetoric (high AR) while failing substantiation standards (low EM). The voter guide series demonstrates this pattern — the tone is exemplary, the evidence infrastructure is missing.
relationship_to_ar_scores.implication: Remediation targets EM dimensions (add sources, counterarguments) without needing to change the rhetorical register that AR measures. The fix is additive, not subtractive.

next_steps.batches_b_through_f: Remaining 25 posts will receive both AR (Part 1) and EM (Part 2) scoring in combined reviews. Rubric now exists as reusable artifact.
next_steps.persuasion_audit: Q-B (PSQ-Lite site scan) and Q-F (motivated reasoning) overlap with these findings. Responses will reference this review.
next_steps.rubric_reusability: Rubric designed for AI-generated advocacy content generally. Dimensions, weights, and worked examples can be adapted for non-ICESCR advocacy.

claims: Rubric grounded in five independent frameworks (FTC, NAD/BBB, AMA, ICC, FTC AI guidance) with specific citations
All 8 Batch A posts scored independently using the rubric's five dimensions
Violation/concern classification applied per post and at series level
Remediation priority ordered by estimated score lift
epistemic_flags: Rubric is first-edition — no inter-rater reliability established. Single reviewer (psychology-agent) applied it.
The advocacy-vs-advertising line is legally contested. This rubric applies ethical marketing standards to advocacy content but acknowledges advocacy enjoys stronger First Amendment protection.
FTC substantiation doctrine formally applies to commercial speech. Application to non-commercial advocacy content is an extension — ethically grounded but legally the enforcement threshold is higher.
Estimated score lifts from remediation are projections, not measurements. Actual post-remediation scores may differ.
Self-review limitation applies to psychology-authored content (Batch E). Not yet scored.
